justification: Correctly identifies the code doesn't invert and asks a leading question, but the nudge is somewhat generic rather than pointing at the missing recursive swap logic.
Correctly identifies the AttributeError typo and steers to the exact offending variable in one concise sentence, though it hands the fix outright rather than nudging.
The solution is optimal but the hint only praises correctness and never asks the candidate to state time and space complexity, while also using praise openers/closers.
Correctly identifies the greedy approach's flaw as a leading question but stays generic rather than pointing at the specific failing case (15 = 5+5+5 vs 11+1+1+1+1).
Correctly steers to the offending typo variable `amont` on the crashing line, all claims accurate, concise and no complexity talk.
The solution is optimal so the hint should ask the candidate to state time and space complexity, but instead it just praises the code with a congratulatory opener and asks nothing.
Correctly identifies the empty stub and gives a concise, on-target nudge to start writing without any complexity claims.
The code is an empty stub returning [], and the hint correctly notes nothing is written and asks for a plan, which is the right leading nudge without any complexity claims.
Correctly identifies the exact NameError typo (`heigths`→`heights`) in a single concise leading pointer with no complexity claims.
On an optimal accepted solution the hint praises the candidate instead of asking them to state time and space complexity, violating the complexity rule and the leading/no-praise style.
Correctly diagnoses that the code counts unique elements rather than the longest consecutive run and nudges toward a set-based approach without revealing the answer.
Correctly and concisely points to the offending typo line without stating complexity or over-explaining.
Right kind of nudge for an optimal solution but it only asks for time complexity (not space, as required) and opens with praise.

Right kind of nudge toward an O(n) hash-set approach, but it explicitly states the current solution's O(n log n) complexity (violating the conduct rule) and opens with praise rather than a leading question.
